title: Pane pugliese
subtitle: Apulian white bread with an open crumb and crisp crust
excerpt: A large, round Apulian loaf with a thick, crackly crust and an open, moist crumb. The dough is wetter than a standard white loaf and is coaxed rather than kneaded, relying on stretch-and-fold passes during the bulk rise to build strength without degassing. Baked in a covered dutch oven for the first part of cooking, which traps steam and gives the crust its characteristic shattering quality.
sourceNotes: Pane pugliese is a traditional white bread from the Puglia region of southern Italy, documented in Italian regional food literature from the late nineteenth century onward. Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, freely available at Project Gutenberg IT), describes the southern Italian bread-baking tradition. The loaf is described in Italian baking guides published before 1928 as a large, round boule baked on a stone or in a wood-fired oven, with an open crumb characteristic of high-hydration doughs.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice before freezing for easier use. Refresh individual slices in a toaster or warm oven.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping, place the boule seam-side up in a floured bowl, cover tightly, and retard in the refrigerator for up to 16 hours. Bake from cold, adding 5 minutes to the covered baking time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 220°C. Dutch oven method: covered for the first 20 minutes to trap steam, then uncovered for 20 to 25 minutes to colour and crisp the crust.

recipeTools.notes: At least 24 cm diameter. Preheat in the oven.

glossaryTerms.definition: A method of developing gluten in a wet dough without kneading on a bench. With the dough in a bowl, reach under one side, pull it up and stretch it as far as it will go without tearing, then fold it back over the top. Rotate the bowl 90 degrees and repeat for all four sides, completing one set. For pane pugliese, three to four sets during the first 90 minutes of bulk fermentation, spaced 30 minutes apart, build enough strength for the dough to hold a rounded shape when baked.

content.text: The wet dough and the covered dutch oven are the two things that make pane pugliese what it is. The high hydration creates the open, irregular crumb; the dutch oven traps steam during the first phase of baking, allowing the loaf to spring up fully before the crust sets. The dough is built with
rather than kneading, which suits the slack texture.

Combine the flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Add the warm water and olive oil and mix with a dough scraper or wet hands until no dry flour remains. The dough will be rough and slack. Cover and leave for 30 minutes, then perform the first set of stretch-and-folds. Repeat three more times at 30-minute intervals. After the final set, cover and leave for the remainder of the 90-minute bulk ferment.

Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface. Gently shape into a round by pulling the edges in toward the centre and flipping seam-side down. Tuck the dough under itself to build tension across the surface. Place seam-side up in a well-floured bowl, cover, and prove for 60 minutes until noticeably expanded.

Place the dutch oven (with its lid) in the oven and heat to 240°C conventional (220°C fan). When at temperature, carefully flip the proved dough into the hot dutch oven seam-side up. Score the surface if desired. Replace the lid and bake for 20 minutes, then remove the lid and bake for a further 20 to 25 minutes until deep golden brown. The loaf is done when it sounds hollow when tapped on the base and reads 94°C in the centre. Cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before cutting.
